text.title: Eviction Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Three failure modes: trusting the model, lossy eviction, and over-eviction that starves the agent.

section.title: Trust the model to summarize
section.p: The most common failure is configuring nothing in the runtime and writing a system-prompt clause asking the agent to compress its own tool outputs.

antipattern.description: System prompt instructs: "If a tool returns more than ~10k tokens, summarize and continue." No middleware enforces it.
antipattern.reason: The full payload is in context the moment the tool returns; the summary that comes after cannot un-pollute earlier turns. Compaction must be a runtime guarantee, not a prompt request.

section.reference: lethain — Building an internal agent: context window compaction (why programmatic enforcement beats prompts).

section.title: Lossy eviction with no recovery
section.p: Some implementations replace the oversized result with a flat `[truncated]` marker and discard the body.

antipattern.description: Middleware truncates tool output to first 500 chars and drops the remainder. No path, no scratch file.
antipattern.reason: The agent later realises it needs line 4,200 of the log and has no way to retrieve it — task fails or burns turns re-running the tool. Eviction without a path is just truncation.

section.title: Over-eviction starves the agent
section.p: Setting the threshold too low makes the agent constantly call `read_file` for trivial results.

antipattern.description: Threshold set to 500 tokens "to be safe". Every tool result becomes a manifest the agent must dereference.
antipattern.reason: Doubles tool calls, wastes turns, raises latency. The threshold must be tuned to median tool result size; LangChain's empirical default of 20,000 is a sane starting point. Measure your own distribution and pick the 90th percentile.

section.reference: LangChain — How agents can use filesystems for context engineering (threshold tuning guidance).
